flashcards.front: What is a Process Group in distributed systems?
flashcards.back: A collection of processes that cooperate to achieve a common goal. Members can send messages to the group as a whole (multicast) and receive messages from other group members.
A multicast protocol where messages are delivered to all processes in a group or to none. It ensures all-or-nothing delivery, providing reliability and total ordering guarantees.
A paradigm where messages sent in a communication step are delivered in the same step to all non-faulty members. The group membership view remains consistent across all correct processes.

flashcards.front: Explain Causal Ordering in group communication
flashcards.back: If a process sends message m2 after receiving message m1, then m2 must be delivered after m1 at all destinations. It preserves the happens-before relationship from distributed computation theory.
If a message m is delivered before message m' at one process, then m must be delivered before m' at all processes. Ensures all receivers see messages in the same order.

flashcards.front: Define the Consensus problem in distributed systems
flashcards.back: Processes must agree on a single value proposed by one or more processes. Requires agreement (all decide same value), validity (decided value was proposed), and termination (all eventually decide).

flashcards.front: What is the Byzantine Agreement problem?
flashcards.back: A consensus problem where faulty processes may behave arbitrarily (send incorrect/wrong messages). Requires reaching agreement despite up to f Byzantine failures in a system of n processes where n > 3f.
A consensus algorithm that achieves agreement despite failures. Uses three roles: Proposer, Acceptor, and Learner. Guarantees safety and liveness in asynchronous systems with failures.
A service that maintains and manages the list of processes in a group. Handles member joins, leaves, and failures while notifying all members of membership changes.
Messages sent by the same process are delivered in the same order they were sent. Each sender's messages maintain their send order but different senders' messages can be interleaved.

flashcards.front: What is a View in group communication?
flashcards.back: A view represents the current membership of a process group. It contains an identifier and the list of current members. Views are installed atomically at each process.
A protocol for achieving atomic commitment in distributed transactions. Coordinator first asks participants to prepare, then if all vote yes, sends commit; otherwise sends abort.
A communication primitive where a message is sent to a group of processes. Unlike unicast (one-to-one) or broadcast (one-to-all), multicast is one-to-many communication.
A multicast protocol that guarantees: (1) validity - any message sent is eventually delivered, (2) agreement - if delivered by one correct process, delivered by all correct processes.

flashcards.front: What are the challenges in group coordination?
flashcards.back: Process failures, message delays, network partitions, asynchronous communication, maintaining consistency, achieving agreement despite faulty components, and ensuring liveness while preserving safety.
